description: Figure 3 presents optical micrographs comparing the microstructures of LN (low-nitrogen) and HN (high-nitrogen) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels after vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1423 K for 2 h, multidirectional hot forging between 1123 and 1323 K, and air cooling. Subfigure (a) shows the LN steel comprising an austenite matrix with coarse, long parallel stripe-like ferrite located at austenite boundaries; the caption states a mean austenite grain size of 20.5 μm and a ferrite area percentage of 5.08%. Subfigure (b) shows the HN steel with a refined austenite matrix and markedly less ferrite; the caption reports a mean grain size of 12.1 μm and a ferrite area percentage of 1.84%. Both micrographs reveal annealing twins intersecting within austenite grains. The comparison demonstrates that nitrogen addition significantly refines austenite grains and reduces ferrite content, consistent with the reported increase in fine deformation twins in HN steel. These microstructural changes support the conclusion that nitrogen stabilizes austenite, suppresses ferrite formation, and thereby enhances ultimate tensile strength, plasticity, and toughness, while the reduced ferrite fraction contributes to the observed slight decrease in yield strength.
